Setting: a bright, warm cottage-style home kitchen with white lower cabinets and warm wood-tone counters, soft natural light from a window, white and wood tones throughout, potted herbs and a bowl of oranges on the sill, a black cast-iron pan on a gas stove with a blue flame.

The chef is Ginger, a real male domestic orange tabby cat with a healthy, well-proportioned average build — a normal balanced adult cat, neither chubby nor bony, with a naturally proportioned face. He has natural realistic fur (individual strands visible), a slightly wet pink nose, lifelike whiskers, and natural green eyes with real reflections, wearing a small white chef's hat and a red-and-white striped apron.
With him is Pepper, a real female black-and-white tuxedo cat (black back and head like a mask, white muzzle/chin, broad white chest, white socks on the front paws, yellow eyes, pink nose), his close best friend, sitting nearby watching. Ginger and Pepper are the same size — equally sized adult cats.

Action: Ginger tosses freshly fried chicken pieces in a glossy red-orange gochujang sauce in the pan, the sauce bubbling and coating each piece. He plates the finished yangnyeom chicken on a white dish and scatters sesame seeds over the top, half-standing with his paws on the counter, never fully upright. Pepper watches the plate a little too intently, licking her lips.

Consistency: all objects stay physically stable — nothing duplicates, merges, morphs, or vanishes. Ginger keeps a healthy well-proportioned average body and balanced face, and wears his hat and apron the whole time; even half-standing he keeps the scale of a REAL normal house cat, never humanoid or enlarged. Ginger and Pepper stay the same size as each other. Pepper always has exactly one single black tail — never splitting or doubling; her mouth and chin are solid clean white fur with no black dots. Both cats' paws always stay natural cat paws — never turning into human hands, fingers, or thumbs.

Audio: real ambient sounds only — the sizzle and bubbling of the sauce in the pan, a soft scatter of sesame seeds. When a cat vocalizes it must be a REAL authentic recorded-sounding domestic cat meow — NOT a human voice imitating a cat. No background music, no BGM, no instrumental music, no melody, no soundtrack of any kind — only real ambient and cat sounds.
